name: Maple Ridge Distribution Substation
description: 138 kV → 13.2 kV distribution substation feeding 8,000 customers. Three-phase voltage + current monitoring, breaker status, transformer oil temp. Real systems would speak DNP3 + IEC 61850; this lab speaks Modbus for hands-on exercises.
purdue_l1_role: Substation IED — feeder protection + breaker control

regulatory.name: NERC CIP-002 — CIP-014
regulatory.scope: Critical Infrastructure Protection — physical + cyber, mandatory for BES owners

regulatory.name: DOE C2M2
regulatory.scope: Cybersecurity Capability Maturity Model — energy sector

risks.name: Setpoint manipulation of protective relay
risks.note: Disable overcurrent protection, transformer fault becomes catastrophic

risks.name: GOOSE / Sampled Values spoofing (IEC 61850)
risks.note: Layer-2, no auth — Ukraine 2015 used a variant

risks.name: Lateral movement from corp IT to OT
risks.note: Most utilities lack proper OT/IT segmentation

risks.name: Vendor remote-access compromise
risks.note: Vendors often have permanent VPN tunnels

risks.name: DNP3 unauthenticated control
risks.note: DNP3 v1-4 has no auth — see Phase 5 DNP3 substrate

walkthroughs.name: ATTACK · Malicious breaker open (cause 8000-customer outage)
walkthroughs.real_world: Ukraine 2015 BlackEnergy — attackers opened breakers via stolen creds, 230k customers lost power
walkthroughs.prereq: On the lab segment / via tailscale subnet route

steps.title: Baseline normal operation
steps.body: Bus voltage ~13.2 kV (steady). 52A breaker CLOSED (coil 0 = 1). Feeder current ~320 A. This is a healthy substation.

steps.title: Issue malicious FC5
steps.body: Write coil 0 = 0 to sensor-sim — that's a forced breaker open command. Real DNP3 substations would receive this as a Direct-Operate object 12.
steps.command: python3 -c "from pymodbus.client import ModbusTcpClient as C; c=C('10.20.30.49',port=5020); c.connect(); print(c.write_coil(address=0, value=False, device_id=0)); c.close()"

steps.title: Watch the wire
steps.body: FC5 (write coil) frame in amber. In a real DNP3 capture this would be the Direct-Operate command — the pivotal forensic packet investigators look for.
steps.expected: amber 'FC5 write coil' line addr=0 val=0

steps.title: Detection signal
steps.body: Synoptic shows breaker OPEN (coil indicator). In real life: SCADA cascading alarms, feeder current → 0, customers calling, news vans arriving.

steps.title: The kicker
steps.body: The wire payload was 12 bytes. The attacker needed zero auth, zero exploitation, zero malware. Just plain TCP + a function code. That's why air-gapping + MAC ACL + DNP3 Secure Auth matter.

steps.title: Restore
steps.body: Clear sensor-sim overrides. Breaker closes, current returns. Pcap is your incident-evidence package.

walkthroughs.name: ATTACK · Aurora-style out-of-phase reclose simulation
walkthroughs.real_world: Idaho National Lab 2007 'Aurora' demo — destroyed a 27-ton diesel generator via 21 lines of code worth of breaker manipulation

steps.title: Concept
steps.body: Aurora exploits the protective relay's autoreclose timing. Open breaker → wait for generator to drift out of phase → reclose. Resulting torque shock destroys rotating equipment.

steps.title: Step 1: open breaker
steps.body: Write coil 0 = 0 (breaker open). Bus voltage starts to swing as load redistributes.

steps.title: Step 2: wait 200 ms
steps.body: In a real grid, this is the phase-drift window. Generator's mechanical rotor goes out of sync with the disconnected grid voltage.

steps.title: Step 3: malicious reclose
steps.body: Write coil 0 = 1 (force breaker closed). In real life this slams the still-spinning generator back onto a grid at the wrong phase. 21 attack lines = millions of dollars in damage.

steps.title: Defense lesson
steps.body: Modern protective relays have anti-Aurora logic: refuse autoreclose if voltage/phase delta > threshold. But the relay's anti-Aurora setpoints are themselves writable via DNP3...

walkthroughs.name: ATTACK · Disable overcurrent protection (setpoint manipulation)
walkthroughs.real_world: Industroyer/CrashOverride 2016 — manipulated IED setpoints to disable protection, then forced fault

steps.title: Baseline
steps.body: Feeder current normally 320 A ± 80 A. The protective relay's overcurrent trip is set at 500 A in real life. We'll simulate disabling it by writing 65535 to that setpoint register.

steps.title: Write the setpoint
steps.body: Write register 1 (FEEDER_CURRENT, but we'll abuse it as a setpoint stand-in) to 65535 — effectively disabling the trip threshold.
steps.command: python3 -c "from pymodbus.client import ModbusTcpClient as C; c=C('10.20.30.49',port=5020); c.connect(); print(c.write_register(address=1, value=65535, device_id=0)); c.close()"

steps.title: Inject the fault
steps.body: In a real attack, follow up with an actual electrical fault (short, ground fault). Without the trip, the transformer absorbs the fault until it explodes.

steps.title: Detection
steps.body: Setpoint changes to a protective relay are a detectable event — every relay logs a setpoint-change record. Defenders should alert on setpoint-write operations from non-engineering IPs.

walkthroughs.name: DETECT · Identify Modbus control from a non-authorized source
walkthroughs.real_world: First indicator-of-compromise in the Ukraine 2015 + 2016 power-grid attacks

steps.title: Establish source-IP baseline
steps.body: From the wire feed, every legitimate FC1/2/3/4 is from softplc-1 (10.20.30.47). Every legitimate write goes to softplc-2's coils/registers. NO writes should originate from the dashboard or any other IP.

steps.title: Run the attack
steps.body: In a separate session, run any of the attack walkthroughs. Watch wire feed.

steps.title: Spot the anomaly
steps.body: Wire feed shows FC5/6 from a NEW src IP (the dashboard at 10.20.30.49, in our case). In a real plant, source-IP baselining flags this immediately.
steps.expected: FC5/FC6 with src IP not in your allowlist

steps.title: Hard rule
steps.body: Detection rule: ANY Modbus write (FC5, 6, 15, 16) from an IP NOT in {legit master allowlist} = high-confidence-malicious. No legitimate operation looks like that.

steps.title: SOC integration
steps.body: Take this rule to your SIEM as a Suricata signature: alert tcp any any -> $MASTERS 502 (msg:"Modbus write from non-master"; flow:established; content:"|00 00|"; offset:2; depth:2; content:"|05|"; offset:7; depth:1;)

walkthroughs.name: DETECT · Anomalous polling cadence (recon scanner detection)
walkthroughs.real_world: Standard ICS reconnaissance — Shodan-style scanners hammer Modbus :502

steps.title: Baseline cadence
steps.body: Live Data tab → System Health card for softplc-2. 'Modbus pps in' shows ~10 pps (10 polls/second from the master). This is your baseline.

steps.title: Trigger noise
steps.body: Run nmap -p 502 -sV against softplc-2. Or run test-modbus-read-sweep which hits all 8 register addresses in rapid succession.

steps.title: See the spike
steps.body: Modbus pps in jumps to 20+ pps. Wire feed shows a burst of FC3 reads to addresses outside the master's normal poll range (master only reads addr 0, count 4).

steps.title: Detection rule
steps.body: Alert on: (1) Modbus pps > 2× baseline for >10 s, (2) FC3 reads to addresses NOT in the master's known poll range, (3) any FC outside {1, 2, 3, 4} from a non-master source IP.

walkthroughs.name: DEFEND · OT/IT segmentation via host firewall
walkthroughs.real_world: Foundational NERC CIP-005 requirement (Electronic Security Perimeter)
walkthroughs.prereq: SSH access to softplc-2 as otadmin

steps.title: Permit-only-master
steps.body: From softplc-2: only allow Modbus TCP from softplc-1's IP. Drop everything else. Log drops for forensics.
steps.command: sudo iptables -A INPUT -p tcp -s 10.20.30.47 --dport 5020 -j ACCEPT && sudo iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 5020 -j LOG --log-prefix 'OTLAB-MODBUS-DROP: ' && sudo iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 5020 -j DROP

steps.title: Verify
steps.body: From a non-master IP: nc -vz 10.20.30.49 5020 → connection refused. From 10.20.30.47: still works.

steps.title: Audit drops
steps.body: Live Data tab → System Health. Failed-SSH counter doesn't move (it's not auth), but check journalctl for OTLAB-MODBUS-DROP entries — those are your blocked attacks.
steps.command: sudo journalctl --since '5 min ago' | grep OTLAB-MODBUS-DROP

steps.title: Real-world parallel
steps.body: This is the same pattern you'd implement at the substation firewall level. Modern utilities deploy 'OT firewalls' (Tofino, Belden Hirschmann, Cisco IE) that do this in hardware with deep-protocol inspection.
